TOO MUCH QUININE.
An Indian Coolie's Good Disposition
Got Him in Bad.
A funny story Is being told by the
manager of an Indian tea plantation
about his efforts to keep his coolies
free from malaria.
He decided tc dose his entire staff
with quinine, and on pay days, as
each coolie came up for his pay, he
administered a fve-grain dose of
quinine, which the coolie had to
swallow on the spot. One night he
was roused out of bed to see one of
the coolies, who was apparently very
ill. He could make nothing of the
case himself, so he sent off at once
for the district doctor.
"What have you been giving him?"
the doctor asked as soon as he saw
the man.
"Quinine," replied the planter.
"How much?" the doctor asked.
"Five grains," was the reply.
"They each had Ave grains."
"Nonsense, man!" the doctor ex
claimed. "He mu have swallowed
fifty grains to be In a state like
this." And bo he had!
Prolonged cross-examination re
vealed the fact that this coolie had
been Induced by some of the others
to come up again and again to the
pay office to draw his own and his
friends' pay, and had taken tho qui
nine each time without a murmur, 60
as not to upset the sahib!

A Disease to Which These Great
Animals Are Peculiarly Susceptible.
Elephants havo good reason to
thank Sir John Dland-Sutton, the fa

They have long been regarded by
man as great, clumsy animals al
most Insensible to pain. Dut as a

of his studies of elephants and their
teeth.
"I have been Interested In this

"At one time I used to make post
mortem examinations of elephants
who died in zoological gardens. I
then found that they sometimes suf

which must have caused them terri
ble suffering. It has also been-found
that the animals may even have
toothache in their tusks, owing to the
formation of abscc-ses.
"A case is on record where an ele
phant went mad in the Strand. After
Its death an abscess was found In
one of its tusks."
One of the keepers of the elephant

"Elephants, you know, keep on
teething all their life. The .top part
of their molars breaks off and the
stump grows again.

The Pour In the Syrian Town of
Hama Will Never Stop,
llama, in Syria, says a writer, is
famed for its huge water wheels.
locally known as naura. There are
four of them', and they are driven
by the river Orontes. which flows
, through the town. Each of the
' wheels bears a distinguished name,
and the visitor to the city is made
aware of their presence long before
j he sees them by the creaking and
! groaning noise which greets his ear.
' At first It suggests a pipe organ and
j later a brass band practicing.
I The wheels are built of a dark
mahogany, which gives them at a,
distance the appearance of iron. The
largest of them boasts of a diameter
of 75 feet and is declared to be the
biggest water wheel tn existence.
The naura are erected or what is
known as the undershot principle r
that Is to bay, they are driven by
the "water striking 'them at "their
base, They serve not only to supply
I the town with water, but also, rrU
j gate the adjacent gardens.
The wheels never stop, summer or
i winter, and day and night their
creaking and groaning are heard. In
the summer months the small boya
may always be semi bathing tn the
river In the neighborhood of the
, wheels, and for a small coin they
' will get In between the spokes of
the wheels and allow themselves to
be carried around many times or
hang on the outside of the wheel

ment is probable.

ADEN, March 22. Thirty-two coal-laden steanlers are here await
ing the Russian fleet.
RUSSIA'S HOME TROUBLES.
ST. PETERSBURG, March 22. There is a recurrence of agrarian
troubles. At Lodz the workmen arc on a strike and one policeman has
been shot. At Mitau agitators stopped work on seventeen estates and
troops were sent to quell them. A bomb exploded at Yarsaw in the
midst of a patrol of police infantry, seriously wounding eight.
o
JULES VERNE DYING.
AURIENS, March 22. Jules Verne is dying of diabetes.
Crushed with disappointment, the most widely read of living French
authors has been slowly passing away for months. Jules Verne is a liv
ing example of the proverb that a
.CQuntrv. The. wonderful stories of
delighted readers of many nationalities have given him a world-wide
reputation and reflected no little honor on his native land. Yet France
has been so ungrateful that she lias denied Jules Verne his one ambi
tion, a seat among the forty immortals of the French Academy. The
disappointment, together with the treatment accorded to him by literary
men of his own country have broken down the old man and he has been
critically ill for months. As an imaginative writer of fiction Jules Verne
has few, of any competitors. He has lived to sec some of his most wide
ly imaginative tales, such as "Around the World in Eighty Days" sur
passed by actual conditions but even amid the wonders of modern'.in
vention and science the charm of the tales of this genius has not less
ened. 0

creased consumption of sugar owing to the high price is the cause.

The following is from the Samoanische Zeitung, published at Apia.
Chinese coolies were introduced in Samoa only about two years ago.
The Chinese coolie Ngo Ah Tshun, number 66, who attempted to
murder A. Schmidt on Christmas eve, by firing two revolver shots at
him, was captured on the 27th inst at Mr. Kramp's plantation, and
brought to Apia in chains. On December 10th this coolie stole M. 535
from Schmidt Bros, plantation at Avele, for which offence he was bound
and confined m one of the rooms of the dwelling house, onjOinstmas
eve, pending the arrival of a policeman to take him toaol. Mr.
Schmidt a little wliile after went to see whether the prisoner was still
safe, and to his surprise he found the coolie had got free of his bonds
and was making off through the window. Mr. Schmidt rushed at the
coolie and managed to get'hold of him. The coblie thereupon drew a
revolver and fired two shots at him, but Mr, Schmidt struck up Ah
Tshun's arm and the shots took no effect. Ngo Ah Tshun was taken
to gaol and finally to the hospital. On the 5th of January he escaped
from the hospital, and has managed to evade arrest up to the 27th inst.
On that date he was captured by the coolies on Mr. Kramp's plantation.
It was known that he was living in the vicinity of Mr. Kramp's place,
in a hut which he had built himself; and that the Chinese there were
supplying him with food. It is supposed that lie was there all the time,
So they were threatened that they would receive no wages, and would
have to work every Sunday until Ngo Ah Tshun was captured. The
Chinese thereupon enticed him into their quarters, overpowered him,
bound him and handed him over to the police,

SHANTUNG BOASTS
\.
From Making Hair Nets to
:Railway Building, Geography
Society States.
"My brain simply reels with all this
? talk about Japan and China and the
, league of nations," pouts dainty Miss
America, as she presses her hands to
' her head, where her rebellious tresses
would be falling down were it not for
a net of human hair?from Shantung.
"Came down here to get away from
.this talk about the peace treaty;
China's a long way off anyhow."
growls Mr. Business Man, as he
_ strolls along: the beach. In a pongee
Teuit?from Shantung.
With this introduction the National
Geographic Society issues a bulletin
concerning the industries of Shan
-tung, which range from making hair
nets to buildinc railways, quoting
from a communication to the society
* by Maynard Owen Williams.
Impetus Given to Industries.
The communication is as follows:
: "When the speedy roadster made
hair nets a necessity the hunger
pressure in a remote province of
jiO.OOO.OOO relented a little.
'The almost invisible net serves
;fashion to preserve for another hour
jthe loveliness of a moment. But the
^making of hair nets enables whole
'Villages of wrinkled old women of
Shantung to put a little more food
?lnto ever-hungry stomachs.
- "When the American male emerged
?from the woolen of former convention
and donned the dapper suit of cool
pongee all the silk worms In Shan
ktung had to work overtime and their
^masters added a strip of pork to the
:family dish. A pongee-clad crowd at
;Bar Harbor means a better fed popu
lation in Weihaiwel.
"An American woman weare some
tChefoo lace, and, thanks to her and
:.the purchases of her friends, almond
-eyed girls are being trained in mis
. ?ion schools S.OOO miles away.
"The doughboy back from the war is
y =

Asaarican Lead Pancil Co., New York
also a booster for Shantung, though
perhaps he doesn't know it. As he
tells of the ever-smiling Chinese
whom he saw making roads in France
he testifies to the fine qualities of
some of the world's best laborers.
Shantung Coolia Factor in War.
"The Shantung coolie did his fair
share of war work. A hundred and
fifty thousand of him went out to
better living conditions and a wider
outlook when the British troopships
steamed away from his peninsular
home. Hundreds of him dropped
shovel and seised run or fought with
clubs and axes when the breach at
Chateau Thierry yawned.
"Now some of those Shantung
coolies are being returned to their
homes with new thoughts and ideals,
speaking pidgin-French, pidgin-Eng
lish and what not, but with wonder
ful tales to tell of the men by whose
sides they fought.
"When China wants railways built
or canals dug here are the boys wfio
showed the best allied engineers what
loyal labor really was.
"Nor wtll they have to wait long.
An American corporation is only
waiting for better transportation fa
cilities before beginning to dredge
once more the grand canal, which was
binding China into an empire two
oenturied before the great wall began
to shut out the rest of the world and
400 years before the birth in a Beth
t lehem manager of Him who was to
aftect Chna in a degree second only
to Shantung's great sage.
"When 30.000.000 people whose idea
of a day's work is sixteen hours are
crowded into a province the size of
Iowa there must either be industrial
development in silk, lace and hair or>
periodic migrations of labor to less
thickly settled parts of the world.
His Summer Vocation.
"In summer the Shantung coolie is
north along the Amur, mining gold or
harvesting soy beans in Manchuria.
I have seen him carrying Harbin
floor aboard the Sungari steamers,
and he laid hundreds of miles of ties
on the Transsiberian. I have seen
him juggling gayly painted sticks at
the Nljnl Novgorod fair, and compa
nies of Shantung coolies fought for
the bolsheviks beside the Kremlin,
and against them near Tchita. In
ruined Van a Shantung coolie, heav
ily dressed against the bitter cold of
the Armenian plateau, rolled into
town ahead of twenty of his com
patriots who brought flour to that
starving city.
"Soon the Japanese will be laying
the rails for their new railway con
cession from Kaomi. near Tslngtau,
to Hauchowfu. whence a transasiatic
trunk line?which will be to the
Transsiberian what the Union Pa
cific is to the Canadian Pacific?is
some day to link Lanchow and Kash
war with Peking and Russian Turke
stan. Another Japanese line will run
from Tsinan to cut the Peking-Can
ton line at Shuntenfu.
"In building these railways the
Shantung coolie will have his rightful
place, and skill gained In France will
stand him in good stead In linking
his home province to the capitals of
Kurasia from Madrid to Tsinan."

bureau here announces that the Polish
army has achieved a complete victory
over the bolsheviki after a ten-day
batle on the Duna river. It is assert,
ed that the bolshevik!, who previously
h?d crossed the Duna, were surround
ed and killed or taken prisoner.
The bureau adds that the Poles have
gone up the Duna and occupied the
town of Duisno.

FLEET BASES SELECTED
|
Recommendations for extensive im
provements of naval facilities on the
Pacific coast will be made to Congress
In the near future, it was announced
at the Navy Department today. Secre
tary Daniels said that as a result of
his? trip to the Pacific coast in con
nection with the arrival there of the
recently created Pacific fleet he wo"'^
soon ask Congress to authorize tne
establishment of additional dry docK,
navy yard and shipbuilding facilities.
Pu^et sound and San Francisco will
be the two chief bases of the Pacific
fleet, Mr. Daniels said. The dread
nauffhts and cruisers will be
about equally between these two
ports, it was said, and the smaller
vessels, including destroyers. ?ul>"
marines, .supply ships and auxiliary
vessels, will be bastd on the smaller
ports of the Pacific coast.
Secretary Resumes Duties.
Secretary Daniels resumed his duties
at the Navy Department this morn
ing after an absence of about six
weeks, during which he cruised along
the Pacific coast and to the-Hawauan
Islands. He arrived in this city last
evening, having made the trip
Seattle, Wash., with only one stop at
Chicago. Accompanying b'm were
Mrs. Daniels. Hear Admiral Charles
W. Parks, chief of the bureau of
yards and docks: Rear Admiral J S.
McKean of the bureau of operation*
Commander James C. Hilton of th
bureau of supplies and accounts and
Commander Percy W. Foote, the Sec
retary's naval aid.
The primary purpose of the trip was
to get first-hand information regard
ing the needs of the Navy for the
better protection of the "matter
with a view to presenting the matter
t0Secretanr Daniels said that his party
had visited every "av,&*b.1? ^ri?hr
from southern California to British
Columbia, and had gathered a maas of
data for use in considering the future
needs of the Navy on the Pacific coast.
Fleet Arouses Enthusiasm.
The Pacific fleet of modern battle
ships had been most enthusiastically
received on the Pacific coast, and Sec
retary Daniels said he and his party
had had a most delightful and Inform
ing experience. .
The passage of the big battleships
through the Panama canal was a won
derful performance and fully demon
strated the great advantages of its
construction from a naval viewpoint.
There will be no more maneuvers
by the Pacific fleet during the com
ing fall and winter, the ? Secretary
said, but target practice and regular
exercises would be continued on all
ships, except those that might be
placed in dock for overhaullug.
In answer to a direct question. Sec
retary Daniels said the fleet had not
been Invited to visit Japan and no
such cruise was In contemplation.
ARMYOF300JBD
Former A. E. F. Staff Chief
Advocates Training of 19
Year-Old Youths.
Maintenance of a peace-time Army
of 100,000 men, with universal mili
tary training of nineteen-year-old
youths, was urged before the House
mLlltary committee today by Maj.
Oen. J. W. McAndrew, commandant of
the War College and former chief of
staff of the American expeditionary
force.
Gen. McAndrew said the Army
should be so distributed as to place
a division of troops in both Panama
and the Hawaiian Islands.
Peace of the Pacific.
"By controlling Panama and Ha
waii," he said, "we will control the
peace of the Pacific on our own terms.
But if we have not such control, then
the peace of the Pacific would be on
the terms of the other fellow."
Chairman Kahn pointed out that
Japan might obtain control of north
Pacific islands formerly held by Ger
many. thus bringing "Japan closer to
Hawaii than ever before, and Rep
resentative McKensle of Illinois asked
If a Japanese attack might not he
made direct on the Pacific coast with
out attempt to capture Hawaii.
In Event of War.
Oen. McAndrew said he believed
that In event of war Japan would
make every effort to take Hawaii, as
possession of the island would he
necessary because of the long line of
sea communication.
In the Philippines Oen. MoAndrew
proposed only a small American force,
augmented by a strong Philippine
militia. Alaska also would be pro
tected by a small body of troops, with
the bulk of the 300,000 men detained
In the United States.
Disagrees With Staff.
Gen. McAndrew, the first of the high
officers of Gen. Pershing's staff to
appear before the committee, said he
was loath to disagree with the gen
eral staff's proposal for a Regular
Army of 509,000 men and 26,000 offi
cers, but contended that 300.000 men
"at the utmost" would be sufficient.
He also disagreed with the general
staff as to the period of universal
training, urging si* months Instead
of three. He added that. "Contrary to
the popular Impression," the average
training period in the American ex
peditionary force was more than six
months.
BOSTON LABOR UNION
OPPOSES QENERAL STRIKE
BOSTON, September 22.?The Bob to ft
Central Labor Union, at a meeting yes
terday held for the purpose of deter
mining Its attitude toward the police
strike In this city, decided against
Calling a general strike at this time.
The Central Labor Union delegates
unanimously accepted the report of
the organisation* strike Committee
that "the tltte U not opportune for
the ordering of a general strike," and
voted moral and financial support to
the policemen's union.
The number of locals pledged to
strike upon the Mil of the central
body was not made public. The city
firemen's union and the carmen's
union, although pledging full sup.
port to thfe policement, aid not strike.

BILITOLMHT
OFAPARTMENTARGUED
The Senate District committee today
gave a hearing on the bill of Senator
Phelan to limit the height of a pro
posed apartment to be built south of
Meridian Hill Park, which, it is
claimed, would obstruct the view
from the park. Col. C. S. Ridley, su
perintendent of public buildings and
grounds. told of his inspection of the
premises and advocated the lowering
of the height of the building by one
story. He said the present plans
would raise the building two feet
above the line of vision from the
park.
W. V. Boyle, one of the owners of
the proposed structure, said the
apartment house will cost about $1,
300,000 and that to cut off one story
would detract from its financial
success. He said that guaranty com
panies had already underwritten the
bonds of the construction company to
the extent of three-quarters of a mil
lion dollars and that the bonds are
widely distributed. The building was
being constructed in accordance with
permits duly issued by the city gov
ernment. He said that the building
obstructs the riew for about 100 feet
in a radius of 1.200 feet, and does
not obstruct at all from the eastward.
Mrs. John B. Henderson urged the
committee to preserve the park,
which, she said, commands one of the
most attractive city view* in the
world.

LONDON. September 21.?An official
wireless dispatch from Berlin, dated
Saturday, says that the Belgian am
bassador at The Hague having be?n
withdrawn the Dutch ambassador at
Brussels also has been withdrawn.
BALTIMORE, Md., September 22.?
When Cardinal Meroier was informed
last night &t Cardinal Gibbons' resi
dence of the reported severance of
diplomatic relations between Belgium
and Holland he said:
"I knew there were difficulties, but
had no reason to suppose a rupture
was Imminent."
Others in his party did not seem
surprised over the news.
Several Reports of Breach.
Several times recently there have
been reports that diplomatic rela
tions between Belgium and Holland
were strained as a result of the de
mands of Belgium for a revision of
the treaty of 1839 between Belgium
and Holland, under which Holland
annexed the southern bank of the
Scheldt river and an elongated strip
of Dutch Limburg lying between
Belgium and Germany. ,
Belgium based her demands upon
the necessity of securing a stronger
military frontier and freeing the
trade of Antwerp, her great port,
from the restrictions now plaoed upon
it by Dutch possession of the lower
portion of the Scheldt, and asked for
political control of the southern bank

PLANS COMPLETED FOR
38th ANNUAL MEETING
OF STATE FEDERATION
'i ,
Many Matters of Importance Scheduled to Be
Aired at Gala Convention to Open in New
Haven Monday Attractive Program
Arranged By Committee In Charge.
Able Orators Secured to Address
Assemblage On Timely
Topics Either Gompers
or Woll to Attend.
Plans have been completed for the 38th annual convention of the
Connecticut Federation of Labor, which opens its four days' session at the
Hotel Garde, New Haven, Monday morning. The gala affair will get under
way promptly at 10 o'clock. Scores of important matters are awaiting dis
position by the delegates. There is every indication that the . convention
will be one of the most important in the history of the enterprising organization.
The convention will open with
prayers offered by the Rev. Martin F.
Doran of St. Francis R. C. church,
New Haven, and the Rev. Oscar E.
Maurer, pastor of Center church, New
Haven. The address of welcome, by
Mayor David E. FitzGerald of the
Elm City, will follow. This is a treat
in itself for the delegates. Practically
everyqne knows the forensic qualities
of the inimitable "Davy Fitz." There
is no doubt that he will have an in
spiring message to deliver.
Patrick F. O'Meara, president of
the Connecticut Federation of Labor,
will be the next speaker. President
O'Meara has prepared an illuminating
address for the delegates. He will
discuss many topics of utmost im
portance. Next will come the annual report
and address of Ira M. Ornburn, sec
retary of the state federation. Be
sides his routine message, Secretary
Ornburn ,as legislative representative
of the Federation of Labor, will ex
plain what this year's legislature did,
or rather did not do, Jpr labor in
terests. It is expected that he will
caustically arraiern the outgoing- lee
islators for their prejudiced stand on
matters affecting labor men.
Samuel Gompers, president of the
American Federation of Labor," has
tentatively agreed to come. In the
event last minute business prevents
him from leaving Washington, he has
arranged to send in his place Matthew
Woll, vice-president of the national
federation and an authority on labor
matters.
The committee has been assured of
the presence of Peter J. Brady, chair
man of the educational committee of
the New York State Federation of
Labor, He will address the conven
tion on the policy of the various trade
unions which have sponsored their
own banks.
Thomas J. Spellacy of Hartford,
Benjamin Slade of New Haven, and
Cornelius J. Danaher of Meriden, are
among the other speakers. All three
are widely known lawyers and elo
quent talkers.
At least one woman speaker will
be on hand to deliver a discourse of
interest to the labor men.
It is expected that the nominations
of officers will be held Tuesday after
noon. The election will take place the
following day. As usual, the Australian-ballot
will be used.
Every effort has been made to cater
to the ne'eds of the delegates during
' their visit to New Haven. Not only '
have ample accommodations been ar
ranged but a smoker, for delegates
only, has been prepared for Wednes
day afternoon at Trades Council hall
on Meadow street. An excellent pro
gram has been arranged for this feature.
Birminghom, Ala., June 1. Alabama
would have a worse reputation than
Florida if the concealed truth about the
methods 'of the convict lease system
were known, declared speakers at a meet
ing held under the auspices of the league
of women voters. The abolition of this
system was demanded.
W. C. Davis, former member of the
Alabama legislature, cited the explosion
in the Banner mine, where scores of
convicts, there against their wills, were
killed.

Almost on the eve of graduation,
William Hutchinson, a Yale senior,
was dropped from college by the
faculty lat week as a result of a
police episode in which he figured.
Several weeks ago, Hutchinson was
arrested on charges of driving an
automobile while under the influence
of liquor. The case was accorded
publicity by the New Haven newspa
pers at the time and naturally came
to the attention of college authorities.
Hutchinson was placed on the car
pet a few days later. , He was unable
to satisfactorily explain his actions.
The drastic ruling followed.
Hutchinson attended Yale for six
years, being an academic and a
school man.
(Special to The Labor News.)
Bridgeport, June 1. -Plans to cur
tail the Saturday working hours of
employes of postoffices, were formal
ly discussed at the annual conference
of the State Association of Letter
Carriers, held here last Sunday after
noon. The matter has been under
consideration for several months. It
affects employes in ever3' postoffice in
the state.
It was suggested, following a brief
discussion, that the proposal be for
warded to the service relation coun
cil at Washington. Clarence Stinson,
of Bayonne, N. J., a member of the
executive board of the council, was
among those present. He promised to
see that the matter received quick at
tention. President J. Gainer of the National
Association of Letter Carriers, and
Congressman Patrick B. O'Sullivan
of Derby, delivered the principal ad
dresses of the interesting sessoins.
The speakers spoke on postal con
dition in general and were (warmly
applauded.
Potmasters Charles W. Birely 01
New Haven, P. L. Shay of Derby,
Charles F. Green of Bridgeport, E.
M. Whitaker of Stamford, and D. M.
Wilson of Hartford, were among
those present.
WRECK NARROWLY AVOIDED WHEN
PINION GEARS IN ENGINE SNAP
Train 43 Stalled F6r Hour When Engine Breaks
Down Near Woodmont Another Example
of Road's Neglect of Important Machinery.
WARS ON LABOR SEEDERS.
Further evidence of the danger con
fronting railroad passengers as a result
of the defective work passed at' the New
Haven road's shops at Readville, ws
evidenced last Tuesday when Train No.
43, leaving New Haven at 12 :30 o'clock,
noon, for New York, broke down near
the Woodmont station narrowly avoiding
being wrecked.
The train was picking up a high rate
of speed when Engineer Graves noticed
something wrong. He hastily brought
the train to a stop. Later investigation
showed that his timely action undoubtedly
averted a serious accident.
It was found that the pinion gears had
snapped, having been worn down to a
point where ordinarily they would have
been noticed before the locomotive was
allowed to leave the yards. The engine
had been passed out as o. k., however,
despite the serious defect.
The breakdown made it impossible for
the train to continue until a phone call
had been sent in and another dispatched
to the scene. The passengers were stall
ed in their cars" for more than an hour
as a' result of the tieup. t
The train was in charge of Conductor
Graves.

McLeod believes the way to hold ne

quire into the cause of their leaving,

The state executive has notified all

or collect from them the state and

Motion Picture Operators' Union,
New Haven, No. 273, is launching a
drive to unionize four nicolets in the
Elm City where men are employed
without cards.
The Winchester theater, on Winchester-'
avenue; the Crystal, on State
street; the Majestic ,on Oak street;
and the Dreamland, on Grand avenue,
are the houses concerned. For some
time, union officials have endeavored
to quietly adjust difficulties with the
managements of these exhibition
places. Overtures have either been
flatly turned down or evaded. As a
.result, a concerted effort is now un
der way to win them over to labor s
side.
The Winchester theater has been
non-union for about two years. The
Crystal, which does a thriving busi
ness in the center of the city, was
union at one time. When salaries
law I were raised, however, the manager
stubbornly refused to act accordingly.
Ever since, thisi nicolet has been
practically a school for non-union op
erators. '
The Dreamland has been ' on the
non-union list of the New Haven
Trades Council for over a year. It
has never been union. The Majestic,
likewise, has always been non-union.
The local has endeavored ' for two
years to sign up the owner of this
house but to no avail.

LABOR AND BANKING
It has taken a long time for the wage earner to learn that
his earnings have for years been used by the capitalists of in
dustry to finance their enterprises and in many instances to
finance the campaigns against the workers themselves, but the
facts are now being realized and arrangements have been com
pleted and are being made for the wage earners to conduct
their own banks and control their own finances.
And unquestioned this is a move in the right direction.
Not that the members or officers of any labor organization
should take over the actual business of running a bank. They
would meet with about the same success a blacksmith would
in repairing a watch. But they should do just what Big Busi
ness does; hire an expert to conduct the business for them.
Many attempts have been made by Big Business interests to
conduct a bank without the hiring of experts and almost with
out exception failure has resulted and in most instances some
one has gone to jail in the bargain.
But the wage earner can hire a banker to conduct the,
business as well as Big Business can hire a banker. Then the
wage earner not only receives the return on his deposits that
he would receive if these deposits were made in any other
bank, but he receives his share of the profits of the banking
transactions as well and he has the further advantage of know
ing that his money is not being used in endeavors to hinder his
efforts to improve his conditions in life.
The time for the wage earner to embark in the banking
business is now.
I. M. ORNBURN.
Hostile Challenge of Cornwall Dignitary, Now a
Superior Court Judge, to Be Taken Up at the
State Convention Next Week Two Speak
ers to Disclose Real Motive of Thrust
Taken at Organized Labor During Ses- 1
sion of Farmers' Legislative Body. '
i i
Leonard J. Nickerson of Cornwall, speaker of the House of
Representatives, and recently appointed superior court judge, who
caused a sensation in union circles a few weeks ago by extolling the
cause of coolie labor, is scheduled to be unmasked during the annual
convention of the State Federation of Labor in New Haven next
week.
.Nickerson s unwarranted outburst
was flaj-ed from one end of the state
to the other at the time. It is to be
officially scored at the big labor as
semblage next week.
During the reading of his annual
report, President Patrick F. O'Meara
is to talk on immigration laws and.
while on the topic he is to disclose
what evidently was behind Nicker
son's advocacy of such labor. Further
insight into the strange attitude of
the well-taken-care-of republican sage
is to be given during the address of
Secretary Ira M. Ornburn. Ornburn
is also the legislative representative
of organized labor and his remarks
concerning Nickerson in particular
are eagerly awaited.
Nickerson's faux pas took place
when he delivered an address before
the farmers' legislative organization.
He said that the contract labor
clauses in the federal immigration
law are wrong and that persons seek
ing labor for farming and other pro
jects in this country should have the
right to 'Send to China and import
coolie labor ,if they so desired.
"The building of the Union Pacific
railroad with coolie labor was one of
the greatest undertakings ever accom
plished in this country," Nickerson
declared. "You ought to have the
right to send abroad and get coolies,
or anybody else, if you want them.
. "The coolie is the best laborer on
earth. He will work hard all day
long and he won't stop at 4 o'clock,
daylight time, or any other kind of
time.
"But they say the Chinese won't
assimilate," he continued. "We don't
want them to. Send them back when
their contract is up. Bring them here
for a limited period, 'say five years,
and then send them back.
"What we want is labor. Labor to
work those Litchfield farmsS The
great need of farmers is labor of some
kind by which he can till his land and
Ave ought to work for it. You do
not realize your power, even on na
tional legislation.'
"Now the labor leaders won't agree
with me on this. They want to con
trol the mechanical branches, of in
dustry, but you cannot get any of
them to work on farms."
Nickerson also explained that Lieut.
Governor Hiram ' Bingham had urged
the abrogation of the immigration
contract labor clauses, describing
Bingham's belief as "hitting the nail
on the head.",
Nickerson's championing of coolies
aroused statewide indignation, par
ticularly among laboring circles. He
has not yet been formally answered
b' organized labor, however but the
answer is coming at next week's "convention.
Only meagre information is avail
able as to what labor's reply will be
to this insolent challenge of the gen
tleman from Cornwall. It is known,
however, that Nickerson will be ac
cused of being merely a mouthpiece
for big business interests, among
other things. An enligktening mess
acre is to be delivered to the delegates'
which will not only disclose Nicker
son in his true light but which will
expose the fallacy of the subtle prop
aganda disseminated by Cornwall'!

Los Angeles, Cal., June 1. In an
address before the City club United
States Senator Ladd of North Dakota
said there is an increasing fear among
the people" that the United States
supreme court is assuming functions
and powers that are alarming and not
contemplated in the constitution
Senator Ladd suggested legislation
under which "no act of congress
should be declared unconstitutional
unless by a vote of eight members
of the court, and no act of any sover
eign state legislature should be de
clared unconstitutional by a vote of
less than seven members.''

GEORGE WASHINGTON
IN TRAFALGAR SQUARE
LONDON. June 80 (Drilled Press).
—A statute of George Washington—
the leader of the Continental
that wrested the thirteen colonies
from George in. —was unveiled to
day In Trafalgar Square, opposite
the National Gallery of art and Just
across the square from a statue of
George IV.
All members of the American of
ficial and residential colony at
tended the ceremony, one of a series
arranged for thU summer In
nectlo'n with the dedication of Sul
grave Manor, homo of Washington's
ancestors.
The staute 1* a reproduction of the
Houdon statue, and was a gift to the
army
I
|
con
British people from the people of
Virginia. John A. Stewart, chair
man of the Sulgrave Institution of
America, was in charge of arrange
ment* for the unveiling.

killed by A. Hughes, a retired rail
road conductor, of Landenberg, Pa.
last March while carrying away a
young pig. The Crosby and Hill
Company had the bird stuffed by W.
H. Hartlsve. The eagle measures
93 Inches from tip to Up.

ON THE PARIS BOULEVARDS. ,
An Extraordinary and Cosmopolitan
Procession.
The crowds that sit before the
Paris cafes on pleasant eevnlngs are
solicited to buy a great variety of
novelties by sidewalks peddlers.
Acrobats appear at short intervals
and do their tricks on carpets laid
on the sidewalks quite as they did in
the days of Quasimodo.
Newsboys (who are always croupy
men, and never boys) thrust before
the idle sojourners at the outdoor^
tables poles to which fold ;d copies
of the evening papers are fastened,
showing the name headings, While
they utter raucous chant, “La Pa
trie!” “La Prusse!"
Now and then an outlander passes
In his. native garb—an Algerian, a
Turk, a Hii.du. a '.I-vican, a Buffalo
Bill Indian, a ; c ..a Da! >mey
—but nobody f- s a note of sur
prise. The enul-i. boulevardier be
trays nu «drnissi a of wonder, :le
Is as sto jal as American aborig
inal
Sensations* women glide by with a
panther treac, each advertising somo
personal ohar-.u by unique shape of
dress or choi :e of orn-ment Their
faces are burlcd In paint, like im
pressionist pictures. Numerous are
children offering sc me popular del
icacy for sale in baskets. It Is what
we call hazelnuts, only green and In
the burr. The boulevardlers shuck
them and eat them unripe.
There Is a low pitched melange of
voices, but you detect no one indi
vidual speech except as now and then
a smiling pair of ic“n stride by, or s
vivacious couple and the ever familiar
twang vibrates with the utterance of
Kansas City or Chicago.
First Aid to the Injured.
Fainting is a loss of consciousness
due to the diminut'un of blood supply
to the brain. It occurs most frequent
ly in weak, sensitive women, but may
also occur to men as well. It usually
occurs In crowds, or In crowded halls,
theaters and churches, where the at
mosphere Is close and the air foul.
Fainting usually lasts only a few
minutes and the person recovers im
mediately when taken out Into the
fresh air; however, there are cases
where It lasts much longer, some
times for an hour or more. The first
aid treatment for fainting is usually
very simple. Take the person out
Into the fresh air, lay them flat on
their hack, with the head lower than
the feet. This can be done by grasp
ing the feet and holding the body so
that the head hangs down, or take an
ordinary straight back chair, turn it
over so that the hack forms an angle
with the floor and place the person
on the back of the chair, with the
head hanging down. This position
with the head hanging down favors
the flow of the blood back to the
brain. All tight clothing about th«
neck and waisf should be unloosened.
Smelling salts or aromatic spirits of
ammonia applied to the nostrils, cold
water sprinkled on the face, chest
and hands, helps to bring the person
Jo.—Dr. H H. Hartung, in National
Magazine.

fWiy Gum Chewing Is a Good Thing.
That the chewing of gum Is not the
V pernicious hahlt it was once consider
ed seems to be a growing opinion
among dentists who have given the
matter careful study. Some eminent
members of the profession even go
ao far as to say that under certain
conditions It Is actually a very bene
ficial thing, particularly for children.
Only 'when the teeth are in their
proper position with each of them
performing Its functions as nature in
tended can a child develop perfectly
and attain the highest standard of
beauty that is inherent to his type.
One tooth slightly out of its natural
place is sufficient to spoil a face which
m|ght otherwise show the most har
; monlouB line®. These defects can
j never be remedied if the childi is fed
on hash, croquettes and other pre
digested food. What he must have
is something more solid which will
I give the muscles of his jaws sufficient
! .exercise and thus force the teeth into
their right places.
Many dentists are advising parents
of children whose faces are too nar
: row and whose jaws are not sufficient
ly developed to make them chew gum.
The constant exercise has a tendency
to widen an otherwise narrow jaw
and thus make room for teeth that
show evidence of crowding each other
i out of place. Chewing gum steadily
for three or four hours a day has most
1 desirable results in changing th«
drwarfed formation of a child’s jaw.
Even when there is no defect of
the teeth or jaw to be corrected, gum
chewing Is beneficial because it exer
cises the muscles of mastication,
thereby improving the digestion and
the consequent nourishment. One
reason why so many people ruin their
health by rapid eating is that as child
ren their jaw muscles never received
enough exercise.
It is quite generally agreed that
provided the gum used is pure and
wholesome, it cannot possibly do
harm either to a child or an adult.
And in many cases it may accomplish
great good not only in developing the
muscles of mastication and aiding di
gestion, but also in keeping the teeth
clean and white. When a child’s
gums are tender and easily Irritated
by the tooth brush, gum chewing be
comes almost a necessity.
POLYGAMY IN TURKEY.
An English Authoress Who Regards
it ?s a Good Thing.
That polygamy Is really a com
mendable Institution for Turkey, and
that it might even be adopted with
profit in England, was the rather
startling declaration recently of Miss
Grace Ellison well-known English
writer, who has just returned from
a protracted visit in and about Con
stantinople.
"It is far better,” Bhe said, "to
have four women supported and pro
tected than to have one well cared
for and three sent out to struggle
and starve under present conditions.
Either put us in harems or give us
freedom and equality with men. Eng
land condemns polygamy without
stopping to consider its advantages
for womanhood, while on the other
hand she turns women adrift in the
world and closes to them all the
professions and trades she can. In
other words, the Englishman won't
support more than one female, and
he won’t help others to support
themselves. Only the other day I
met a woman of 65, the old maid
daughter of a country parson, a semi
invalid, unfitted for work. She hadn’t
a single soul in the world who was
disposed to support or even material- j
ly aid her. Such a thing could not j
happen in Turkey, which w,e think j
so benighted. A man supports all j
female relatives, if necessary, as well j
as his wives.”
TOO MUCH QUININE.
An Indian Coolie’s Good Disposition
Got Him In Bad.
A funny story is being told by the
manager of an Indian tea planMtion
about his efforts to keep his coolies
free from malaria.
He decided to dose his entire staff
with quinine, and on pay days, as
each coolie came up for his pay, he
administered a five-grain dose of
quinine, which the coolie had to
swallow on the spot. One night he
was roused out of bed to see one of
the coolies, who was ’“parently very
ill. He could make .othing of the
case himself, so he sent off at once
for the district doctor.
“What have y u been giving him?”
the doctor asked as soon as he saw
the man.
“Quinine,” replied the planter.
“How much?” the doctor asked.
“Five grains,” was the reply.
"They each had five grains.”
"Nonsense, man!” the doctor ex
claimed. “He must, have swallowed '
fifty grains to be in a state like
this.”
And so he had!
Prolonged cross-examination re
vended the fact that this coolie had
been induced by some of the others
to come up again and again to the 1
pay office to draw his own and his
friends' pay, and had taken the qui
nine each time without a murmur, so
as not to upset the sahib!

It Is said that the world Is very evil
and that there* are no kind souls. ...
You shall see.
On the 28th of last October several
newspapers published the following
advertisement:
"The Under of a purse containing a
$20 bill will kindly return it to No. 9
Lueiente Street, for it was lost by a
poor servant, who cannot reimburse
her employer.’*
* * * *
Well, sir, the following day at 12
o’clock the lackey of an aristocratic
family presented himself at No. 9 Lu
ciente Street witli a purse in his hand.
The door was opened by Itaimonda,
a good looking but very dirty servant,
be it said without offense.
"Is it here that they have advertised
the loss of a purse?”
"Oh, yes, sir.”
"Here it is. Her grace, the mar
chioness of Roble, found it. You may
now rest easy.”
"But ..."
"Good-by!”
And the lackey ran downstairs, mak
ing a great noise, and Raimonda stood
still, staring at the purse . . • •
which was not hers!
How could it be hers?
She had lost an old, worn and
greasy one, and they had left a new
one of green leather, beautiful . . .
She opened it . . . and found a
$20 bill . . . and a $5 bill!
Her first impulse whs to go to the
alcove where her mistress, a paralyz
ed* old woman, was living. She lived
on a government pension of $20. -The
$20 she had given to have changed the
day before yesterday to the servant.
The servant had lost it (as the read
er saw by the advertisement that I
copied), and, returning in tears, had
heard the old woman say:
“Manage it as you please, but I
want my money!"
It was, indeed, all the poor woman
had to live on a whole month!
On that account Raimonda, spend
ing more than she could afford, ad
vertised the loss in three newspapers
of large circulation.
Now that she had the money and
more, the ingrate, forgetting the kind
ness shoirn her by some charitable
soul, was thinking how she could hide
what she had received—for human na
ture is perverse—when the bell rang
once more. Raimonda opened the door, j
and stood facing a nun.
“Is it here that a servant has lor |
a purse?”
"Yes, ma am.
“With twenty dollars?"
“With twenty dollars, a -d a i
munion ticket.”
“Very well; here it is. The dache s
Del Valle found it, anu charged me to
return it to the owner.”
“I am the one.”
“Well, take it; and peace be in this
nouse.”
The nun turned around, and the ras
cal, Raimonda, let her go!
She opened the purse, which was
also new, of Russian leather and de
lightfully perfumed, and found inside
a twenty-dollar bill and a dollar.
Raimonda was dancing for joy in
the kitchen. Her mistress was ring
ing th$ beU> pulling the rop§ attached
to the head of her bed, and crying:
“Raimonda-a-a!"
“Ma’am!”
“Who was here?”
“The water-carrier and the charcoal
man. ’
“With whom were you talking?”
“With them!”
“Bring me a cup of camomile tea.”
"Yes, ma’am.”
The door bell rang while the ser
vant was preparing the infusion. She
put the cup on the table and ran to the
door.
A little old man, very clean and
•well-dressed, was there, and asked:
“Are you the servant?”
"Yes, sir.”
“Are you the one who has lost a
purse ?”
"Yes, sir.”
“Have you suffered on account of
that loss?”
“Yes, indeed! It is the monthly
pension of my mistress, and if 1 do
not give her the twenty dollars, she
will have nothing to eat!”
“Well, then . . . take this . .
here is forty dollars, twenty lor your
mistress and twenty for you.”

of 1818 and the treaty of Washington
of 1846 that the .boundary between
the United States lad Canada from
the Lake of the Wood* to the Pacific
*—ia distance of 1,270 miles—should
follow the forty-ninth parallel. The
surveyors who have lately completed
the location of the boundary have
found that the astronomical parallel
varies from a dlreot olrole around the
earth, and Prof. Otto Klota explains
the curious fact that the line Instead
of being straight, Is at one point 800
feet south of the direct circle, and
at another place 600 feet north. It

Fad for Clean Money.
! There is a fad at present for dis
daining dirty money and seeing to It
that one’s purse contains only bills
and coins that are Immaculate.
Fresh, clean bills can be procured
from the bank, where soiled ones
will be taken In exchange, but clean
silver Is another matter, and those
who follow fads must either accom
plish it themselves or by Instructing
their maids to do It for them. The
silver Is washed In a strong solution
of soapsuds and ammonia, scrubbed
with a brush and polished with a
chamois, the result being glistening
coins In cleanly enough condition to
suit the most fastidious.
Savage’s Love of Medicine.
Mrs. F. Rowling, medical mission
ary at Gayaza, Uganda, tells many
stories In the Strand Magazine about
her patients and their Implicit faith
in the skill and knowledge of "the
White medicine-woman.'* What she
has to say on the matter is as amus
ing as it is Interesting and instruct
ive, and serves to illustrate a curious
trait of the savage mind that has
been commented upon by travelers in
practically all parts of the world—
namely, the keen delight which na
tives' take In sampling the white
man’s drugs. It nyitters .not whether
the subject be well or 111, or whether
the medicine be palatgjjlo or nauseous
medicine they must have It if it is
to he obtained.
“The native patients,” writes Mrs.
Rowling, “are most amusing in the
Implicit faith they have In the musao’
(doctor), as they call Miss Polgrim,
the dispenser here. Anything she
likes to give them they take, but
many of them cannot yet see how a
pain in the head can be cured by
swallowing a pill, and would much
prefer to rub the pill on the place
affected. One day we visited a
woman with a bad pain in her ear.
We gave her two pills to swallow,
but Instead of doing so she proceed
ed to drop them into her ear. An
other tried to put her medicine up
her nostrils!"
DOGS IN GERMANY.
Some Are Harnessed to Four-Wheel
Trucks and Work With a Man.
The dachschund Is the favorite dog
In Germany, and ft very useful and
pleasant companion he seems to be.
The English bulldog is a good second
in popularity, and the nearer he is to
our standard points the better he is
liked. The fox terrier varies a good
deal in size and marking and is evi
dently not so well known in Ger
many as he deserves to be.
The ladies there are as fond of
their little Pojns and their King Char
leses as are their English sisters of
their pet dogs at home.
Some few dogs in the streets are (
harnessed to four-wheeled trucks.
Usually a man and a dog pull. The
work seems too heavy and exhaust
ing for the dogs, which are generally
mongrels of the mastiff type. They
look in fair condition and are not ill
treated.
THE PRICE OF PROGRESS.
Every Great Invention Brings With
It a New Evil.
Apparently every new Invention
brings with it some evil. The British
Medical Journal points out that
tmong distinctively modern diseas
s are the poisoning produced by the
fumes of calcium carbide of acety
lene; the headache, dyspesia, cardiac
failure and sensory disturbances
traceable to the manufacture and use |
of aniline dyes; the frothing of the
blood, known as .caisson disease,
which follows too rapid decompres
sion in workers at the foundations
of bridges; the functional neurosis
known as telegrapher’s cramp, re
sulting from the use of the Morse j
key, and the numerous affections of
the skin, lungs, digestive tract and
eyes due to irritation by organic or
inorganic dusts of Industrial origin.
Transparent carborundum crystals
are now made of high luster and re
fractive power. Although they re
semble diamonds in general appear
ance, they are so brittle that they
cannot be cut and polished. If that
defect is overcome, there is said to
be no reason why the carborundum
gem should not make Its appearance.

j Why the Cuckoo Builds No Nest, i
A Danish legend about the cuckoo
Is very curious. It relates that when
in the eaVly spring time its voice vls
first heard in the woods every village
girl kisses her hand and asks the
question: “Cuckoo, cuckoo, when
shall I be married?” And tho old
people, borne down with age and
rheumatism, ask: “Cuckoo, cuckoo,
when shall I be released from this
world’s cares?” The bird in reply
continues to sing "Cuckoo!” And as
many times as he sings they count
one year to the fulfillment of their
desires;
It is said that the poor bird has so
many questions put to her that be
fore she realizes it the building sea
son has gone by and she is forced
to. put her eggs Into the hedge-spar
rows nest.
Vacuum for Cleaning Horses.
The vacuum machine as used for
cleaning the furnishings of homes
and office buildings has become a
familiar sight in nearly all parts of
the country, but such a machine used
for cleaning horses is new to most
people. The apparatus consists of a
vacuum pump, an electirc motor or
gasoline engine, a steel vacuum tank,
a vacuum gauge containing a separ
ating diaphragm, a number of vacuum
combs and various connecting pipes
and tubing.
A horse can be thoroughly cleaned
by the machine in from three to five
minutes.
